items.title: Use log-domain scoring for numerical stability
items.avoid: Multiplying raw probabilities across dozens or hundreds of features.
items.prefer: Score each class with log P(y) + Σᵢ log P(xᵢ|y); argmax in log space equals argmax in probability space.
items.why: Floating-point underflow zeroes out likelihood products long before log scores become indistinguishable.

items.title: Apply Laplace (add-one) smoothing for discrete features
items.avoid: Leaving zero counts in P(xᵢ|y), which zeroes the entire class posterior for any unseen feature value.
items.prefer: Add α (often 1) to counts: P(xᵢ=v|y) = (count(v,y)+α) / (count(y)+α·V) where V is the vocabulary size.
items.why: Real text and categorical data always produce held-out values; smoothing keeps all classes reachable.
items.example: If "crypto" never appeared in ham training, unsmoothed P(crypto|ham)=0 kills ham for any email containing "crypto".

items.title: Verify the assumption against your feature design
items.avoid: Blindly applying naive Bayes when known interactions drive the label (negation, phrase pairs, XOR patterns).
items.prefer: Engineer interaction features, use n-grams, or switch to models that capture dependencies when correlations given y are strong.
items.why: Independence is an approximation; performance degrades when the product misrepresents class-conditional structure.
items.example: Sentiment with "not good" may need bigram features or a model beyond per-word independence.
